pattern: Simulate day by day: flood-fill every infected region to learn the uninfected cells it threatens and the walls it needs, quarantine the single most threatening region, then let the rest spread, accumulating walls until nothing can grow.
hints: Each day, identify the connected infected regions and, for each, the set of distinct uninfected neighbour cells it would infect next and how many wall segments enclose it.
Quarantine only the one region threatening the most distinct uninfected cells, adding its wall count to the running total and freezing it so it can never spread.
Then advance one night: every still-active region infects its uninfected neighbours; repeat until no active region threatens any new cell.

edge_inputs.description: two regions quarantined over two days
ring enclosing one safe cell needs four walls
left region contained, right keeps spreading
single infected cell needs four walls
single uninfected cell threatened from one side
fully infected grid needs no walls

stress.description: small sparse grids with a unique most-threatening region by construction
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    # build well-separated single-cell or small blobs so threat counts differ and there is no tie
    rows = rng.randint(3, 7)
    cols = rng.randint(6, 12)
    grid = [[0] * cols for _ in range(rows)]
    # place a few isolated infected cells separated by gaps of >=2 to avoid merging and ties
    placed = []
    attempts = 0
    threats = set()
    while len(placed) < rng.randint(1, 3) and attempts < 50:
        attempts += 1
        r = rng.randint(0, rows - 1)
        c = rng.randint(0, cols - 1)
        if all(abs(r - pr) + abs(c - pc) >= 3 for pr, pc in placed):
            placed.append((r, c))
    # give each isolated cell a distinct number of open neighbours by trimming border placement is implicit
    for (r, c) in placed:
        grid[r][c] = 1
    return [grid]
stress.note: isolated single cells keep regions separate; distinct neighbour counts avoid ties
